messages.content: edit for me. Figures 2b and 2c also indicate that the intensity and position of the transmission peak and reflection hole is influenced by the GSST phase state. For the fully amorphous case, the intensity of the transmission peak and reflection hole is significantly higher compared to the fully crystalline state of GSST. This discrepancy may be attributed to changes in the refractive index of GSST material between the amorphous and crystalline phases. The position of the transmission peak and reflection hole also slightly shifts towards longer wavelengths as the GSST phase state transitions from fully amorphous to fully crystalline.

Figures 2b and 2c further illustrate how the intensity and position of the transmission peak and reflection hole are affected by the phase state of GSST. It is observed that in the fully amorphous state, the transmission peak and reflection hole exhibit significantly higher intensity when compared to the fully crystalline state of GSST. This divergence in intensity can be attributed to alterations in the refractive index of the GSST material between its amorphous and crystalline phases. Additionally, as the GSST phase state progresses from fully amorphous to fully crystalline, there is a slight wavelength shift towards longer wavelengths in both the transmission peak and reflection hole.

Fig. 3. a) The amount of the a) transmission and b) reflection of the TM defect mode as a function of the crystalline fraction of GSST (m). Here, λ indicates the wavelength corresponding to the TM defect mode.

Figure 3a depicts the variation in the transmission of the TM defect mode, while Figure 3b illustrates the changes in the reflection of the same mode, both as a function of the crystalline fraction of GSST denoted by "m". The wavelength associated with the TM defect mode is denoted by λ.

please improve my text i want to be formal, native form and the most coherence between sentences. my text is  : In the following, we have constrained our calculations for optical phenomena with the TM polarization due to the fact that the defect mode (transmission peak and reflection hole) only manifests in the spectrum of waves with TM polarization (see Figure 2). 
The GSST phase state plays a pivotal role in modulating the optical behavior of the structure, leading to changes in the transmission, reflection, and absorption properties of the structure, which may be important for the design of photonic devices based on phase change materials with tunable optical properties. To elucidate the effect of altering the state of the GSST defect layer from fully amorphous to completely crystalline and intermediate states on the behavior of the defect mode, we have plotted the spectra of (a) transmittance and (b) reflectance of the defect mode as functions of the crystallinity ratio (m) and wavelength in Figure 3. In accordance with Figure 2, Figure 3 illustrates that the intensity of the defect mode transmission (reflection) diminishes (increases) as m increases from 0 to 1. Furthermore, as m increases, both the transmittance and reflectance peaks of the defect mode shift towards longer wavelengths. Thus, this figure clearly indicates that the intensity and position of the defect mode can be effectively controlled by altering the state of the GSST layer.

In our investigation, we have focused on optical phenomena involving TM polarization. This choice was made because the defect mode, characterized by a transmission peak and reflection hole, exclusively manifests in the spectrum of TM-polarized waves, as demonstrated in Figure 2.

The phase state of GSST plays a crucial role in shaping the optical behavior of the structure. It governs the transmission, reflection, and absorption properties of the structure, which hold significance in the design of photonic devices leveraging phase change materials with tunable optical properties.

To examine the impact of varying the GSST defect layer from a fully amorphous state to complete crystallinity, as well as intermediate states, on the behavior of the defect mode, we have created spectra illustrating the (a) transmittance and (b) reflectance of the defect mode. These spectra are plotted as functions of the crystallinity ratio (m) and wavelength in Figure 3. In line with the findings of Figure 2, Figure 3 reaffirms that the intensity of the defect mode's transmission decreases as m increases from 0 to 1, while its reflection increases. Furthermore, as m increases, both the transmittance and reflectance peaks of the defect mode exhibit a shift towards longer wavelengths. Therefore, this figure serves as clear evidence that the intensity and position of the defect mode can be effectively manipulated by modifying the state of the GSST layer.

please help me edit my text i want to chang and in=moroveand i want to be most coherence between sentences.To systematically investigate the influence of period number of the 1DPCs on the optical properties of the defect modes, we plot Fig. 6 in the both a) amorphous and b) crystalline phases for different period number. These plots show the spectra of the defect mode's transmittance, reflectance, and absorption as functions of wavelength in the vicinity of the defect mode wavelengths (). The parameters utilized in this figure are analogous to those in Figure 2, except for the number of periods (N), which is varied at , , and . Comparing Figure 6a to 6b reveals that the resonance peak, reflectance dip, and absorption peak of the GSST defect mode in amorphous and crystalline states exhibit distinct behaviors with increasing period number. In the amorphous state, as seen in Figure 6a, the resonance peak not only sharpens but also decreases in intensity with higher N. The intensity of the reflectance dip, however, remains independent of N and only sharpens with increasing photonic crystal period. As for the absorption peak in the amorphous state, it behaves similarly to the previous cases, sharpening with increased N, but in contrast to the other cases (transmittance and reflectance), its intensity increases with higher N. In the crystalline GSST state (see Figure 6b), an increase in N leads to consistent effects on the resonance peak, reflectance dip, and absorption peak. As shown, increasing N sharpens these modes and reduces their intensities.

To comprehensively explore the impact of the period number of 1DPCs on the optical properties of defect modes, we present Figure 6, which includes both the amorphous (a) and crystalline (b) phases for different period numbers.

In these plots, we depict the spectra of transmittance, reflectance, and absorption for the defect mode as a function of wavelength, specifically in the vicinity of the defect mode wavelengths. We maintain similar parameters as Figure 2, except for the number of periods (N), which we vary at
, , and .

Comparing Figure 6a to 6b reveals distinctive behaviors of the resonance peak, reflectance dip, and absorption peak of the GSST defect mode in amorphous and crystalline states as the period number increases. In the amorphous state shown in Figure 6a, the resonance peak becomes sharper and experiences a decrease in intensity with higher N. However, the intensity of the reflectance dip remains unaffected by N and only sharpening occurs with an increasing photonic crystal period. Interestingly, the absorption peak in the amorphous state follows a similar trend, sharpening with higher N, but in contrast to transmittance and reflectance, its intensity actually increases as N increases.

Moving on to the crystalline GSST state depicted in Figure 6b, an increase in N consistently results in sharper modes for the resonance peak, reflectance dip, and absorption peak. Concurrently, their intensities decrease as N increases.

To comprehensively explore the impact of the period number of 1DPCs on the optical characteristics of defect mode, we plot Figure 6, which includes both the fully amorphous (a) and fully crystalline (b) phases of GSST defect layer for different period numbers. These plots show the spectra of the defect mode's transmittance, reflectance, and absorption as functions of wavelength in the vicinity of the defect mode wavelengths ( ) . The parameters utilized in this figure are analogous to those in Figure 2, except for the number of periods (N), which is varied for N=5, N=7, and N=10.

To thoroughly examine the impact of the period number of 1DPCs on the optical characteristics of the defect mode, Figure 6 is presented. This figure encompasses both the fully amorphous (a) and fully crystalline (b) phases of the GSST defect layer, focusing on different period numbers.

Figure 6 displays the spectra of the defect mode's transmittance, reflectance, and absorption, specifically in the vicinity of the defect mode wavelengths ( ). The parameters employed in this figure align with those used in Figure 2, with the exception of the number of periods (N), which varies in this case with values of N=5, N=7, and N=10.

Comparing Figure 6a to 6b reveals that the resonance peak, reflectance dip, and absorption peak of the GSST defect mode in amorphous and crystalline states exhibit distinct behaviors with increasing period number.

A comparison between Figure 6a and 6b highlights the distinct behaviors exhibited by the resonance peak, reflectance dip, and absorption peak of the GSST defect mode in both amorphous and crystalline states as the period number increases.
